fact_text: Mixed farming exists in many forms shaped by external factors (weather, market prices, political stability, technological developments) and internal factors (soil characteristics, family composition, farmers' ingenuity).

Farmers may choose mixed enterprises to save resources by interchanging them on-farm; this can enable wider crop rotations, reduce dependence on chemicals, align with nature-based preferences, and diversify risk.
Even pastoralists can practice mixed farming because their livelihoods depend on managing different feed resources and animal species.
Mixed farming can include mixed cropping (e.g., multiple crops or varieties with different life cycles on the same field) to use space efficiently and spread risks.
Three major mixed-farming categories are distinguished: on-farm vs between-farm mixing; mixing within crops and/or animal systems; and diversified vs integrated systems.

Modes of farming are described by land, labor and input availability: expansion agriculture (EXPAGR), low external input agriculture (LEIA), high external input agriculture (HEIA), and new conservation agriculture (NCA).
On-farm mixing refers to mixing on the same farm; between-farm mixing refers to exchanging resources between different farms.
In integrated crop-livestock systems, crops provide fodder (grass, legumes, leys, weeds, residues) while animals provide draught and manure and serve as a savings account.
In mixed systems, maximizing the combined yield of components can be more important than maximizing the yield of any single component.
Mixed farming can be poverty-induced; resource-poor farmers may rely on labor-intensive techniques and, with low purchasing power, may overexploit the environment.

Livestock products in mixed systems largely come from animals fed local resources (pasture, crop residues, fodder trees/shrubs) and village-industry by-products (oil extraction, grain milling, cane crushing).
Recycling these resources saves money and reduces waste within the farming system, can create employment, and can improve soil texture and fertility while incentivizing multipurpose animals.
Integrating crops and livestock intensifies organic matter and nutrient cycling; crop residues are the main on-farm source of organic matter and nutrients.
Livestock act as capital assets for security and savings, provide cash income, and are part of nutrient flows; residue management is closely tied to animal feeding.
Integration adds value to crop residues (especially legumes) through animal products and income.
Long-term maintenance requires external nutrient inputs; as feed demand grows, cropland per capita shrinks, and crop yields decline, integration faces new constraints.
In mixed systems, livestock can use products not exploited by humans, such as kitchen waste, roadside/wasteland grasses, and crop residues.
Animals provide multiple outputs (meat, eggs, milk, fiber, social status, income) and dung/urine that fertilize gardens, fields, and fish ponds.
Traditional animal-keeping systems adapt to changes such as population pressure, fertilizer use, and consumption patterns; knowledge exchange helps develop useful technologies.

Mixed-farming systems are livestock systems where more than 10% of dry matter feed comes from crop by-products or stubble, or more than 10% of total production value comes from non-livestock activities.
Rain-fed mixed-farming systems (MR) are mixed systems where more than 90% of non-livestock production value comes from rain-fed land use.
Irrigated mixed-farming systems (MI) are mixed systems where more than 10% of non-livestock production value comes from irrigated land use.
Landless monogastric systems rely on monogastric species (mainly chickens and pigs) fed from outside the farm, separating feed-use decisions from feed production and making nutrient flows open.
Mixed-farming systems contribute the largest share of total meat production globally (53.9%).
More than 90% of global milk production is generated in mixed-farming systems.
Developed countries and Asia together contribute about 70% of total meat production from mixed-farming systems.
Mixed farming systems exist where crop and livestock production occur in the same locality and ownership of crops/land and livestock is integrated.
Specialized livestock in the same locality may still be included as mixed systems if resource-sharing occurs, even with separate ownership.
Some mixed farming systems use farm trees for fodder.
In the semi-arid zone (SAZ), no smallholder farming systems were found lacking a livestock component.
Livestock provide investment capital and wealth creation for households, including for women.
Livestock provide recurrent income, manure for nutrient recycling, energy for traction/transport, and productive uses for farm residues.
Livestock offer a least-cost route to intensification through nutrient cycling, especially when inorganic fertilizers are costly.
Mixed farming can provide more productive use of scarce land, more defensible access to resources, a diversified and resilient household economy, and investment funds to rebuild livestock holdings.

Mixed-farming systems involve crops, livestock and/or trees; livestock systems qualify when more than 10% of animal feed dry matter comes from crop by-products/stubble or more than 10% of total production value comes from non-livestock activities.
Mixed crop-livestock systems provide livelihoods for about one billion people.
Mixed crop-livestock systems produce about half of the world's cereal.
Mixed crop-livestock systems produce around a third of the world's beef.
Mixed crop-livestock systems produce around a third of the world's milk.
Market orientation and strong, growing demand for food provide incentives for sustainable intensification of crop and livestock enterprises in smallholder mixed systems in Africa.
Better exploitation of the mutually reinforcing nature of crop and livestock systems can support positive, inclusive growth that is ecologically and economically sustainable.
Livestock intensification is often neglected relative to crops, even though livestock can raise the productivity of the entire farming system.
Intensifying crop production can benefit livestock and improve natural resource management via increased biomass availability.
Improved efficiency of livestock production means fewer greenhouse gases per unit of milk and more milk per unit of water.
Greater investment in research for development is needed to identify win-win outcomes and enabling policies, technologies, and institutions for sustainable mixed crop-livestock systems.
Share of crop farms among EU farms was 43% in 2005.
Share of crop farms among EU farms was 58% in 2020.
Share of mixed cropping farms was 30% in 2005.
Share of mixed cropping farms was 19% in 2020.
Share of livestock-specialized farms was 24% in 2005.
Share of livestock-specialized farms was 22% in 2020.
The Mixed Farming System in Central Europe covers an estimated 85 million hectares, mainly in the moist subhumid agro-ecological zone.
Rural populations are estimated at about 25-35% of total population and are declining.
Agricultural population in the system is about 16 million.
Most of the 35 million ha of crop area is dedicated to wheat, maize, oil crops and barley, with smaller areas of fruit and vegetables.
There are an estimated 14 million head of cattle within the system.
Total population of the farming system is about 99 million.
Rural population within the system is about 36 million.
Tree Crop Mixed Farming System total area is 85 million ha.
Tree Crop Mixed Farming System agricultural population is 30 million.
Tree Crop Mixed Farming System cultivated area is estimated at 18 million ha.
Tree Crop Mixed Farming System irrigated area is little more than 12 percent.
Tree Crop Mixed systems include rubber, oil palm, coconut, coffee, tea and cocoa, with associated crops such as pepper and spices.
Poverty in the Tree Crop Mixed Farming System is extensive, varying from moderate to very severe.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System total area is 314 million ha.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System agricultural population is 310 million.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System cultivated area is 75 million ha.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System irrigated area is just under one quarter of cultivated area.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System supports about 52 million large ruminants.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System supports about 49 million small ruminants.
Poverty in the Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System is moderate to severe.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System total area is 89 million ha.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System agricultural population is 47 million.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System cultivated area is 8 million ha.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System irrigated area is about one fifth of cultivated area.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System provides grazing for about 16 million large ruminants.
Poverty in the Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System is moderate to severe.
Temperate Mixed Farming System total area is 99 million ha.
Temperate Mixed Farming System agricultural population is 162 million.
Temperate Mixed Farming System cultivated area is 31 million ha.
Temperate Mixed Farming System irrigated area is about one-third of cultivated area.

Major crops in the Temperate Mixed Farming System include wheat and maize, with smaller areas of rice, cotton, soybeans, sweet potato and rape; livestock such as cattle, pigs and poultry are important.
Poverty in the Temperate Mixed Farming System is moderate.
Tree Crop Mixed Farming System accounts for 5% of regional land area and 3% of agricultural population.
Upland Intensive Mixed Farming System accounts for 19% of regional land area and 27% of agricultural population.
Highland Extensive Mixed Farming System accounts for 5% of regional land area and 4% of agricultural population.
Temperate Mixed Farming System accounts for 6% of regional land area and 14% of agricultural population.
In the Rice-Wheat Farming System there is significant crop-livestock integration, with about 119 million bovines and 73 million small ruminants.
Rice-Wheat Farming System total area is 97 million ha with about 62 million ha cultivated.
Rice-Wheat Farming System cultivated area is around 78 percent irrigated.
Rice-Wheat Farming System agricultural population is about 254 million.
Highland Mixed Farming System total area is 65 million ha with about 19 million ha cultivated.
Highland Mixed Farming System cultivated area is around 14 percent irrigated.
Highland Mixed Farming System agricultural population is nearly 53 million.
Highland Mixed Farming System includes about 45 million bovines and 66 million small ruminants.
Poverty in the Highland Mixed Farming System is between moderate and extensive.
Rainfed Mixed Farming System total area is 147 million ha with about 87 million ha cultivated.
Rainfed Mixed Farming System cultivated area is around 16 percent irrigated.
Rainfed Mixed Farming System agricultural population is about 226 million.
Rainfed Mixed Farming System includes about 126 million bovines and 64 million small ruminants.
Poverty in the Rainfed Mixed Farming System is extensive and becomes more severe after droughts.
Highland Mixed Farming System accounts for 12% of regional land area and 7% of agricultural population.
Rainfed Mixed Farming System accounts for 29% of regional land area and 30% of agricultural population.
